title: Regulator/rectifier failure — shared sport bike weakness
description: The CB750, CB919, CB1000R, and Hornet all use the same shunt-type regulator/rectifier from their CBR donor engines. The naked bike configuration actually makes this WORSE because the exposed engine generates more heat with less fairing-directed airflow over the regulator.
fix_procedure: 1. Test: engine at 5000 RPM, 13.5-14.5V across battery. 2. If out of range, test stator AC output. 3. Replace with MOSFET regulator. 4. Hard-wire stator connector. 5. Forum tip: the naked bike layout means the regulator sits in the engine's heat plume with no fairing to direct airflow. On the CB919 and CB1000R, consider relocating the MOSFET regulator to the tail section or under the seat where it gets more air. The CB750 (Nighthawk) has the regulator behind the engine — worst possible spot. Same MOSFET upgrade applies to all models.

title: Cam chain tensioner noise — inline-4 models
description: The CB919 (CBR900F engine), CB1000R (CBR1000RR-derived engine), and Hornet 600 (CBR600F engine) all inherit the Honda inline-4 CCT weakness. The standard bike RPM profile (more time at low RPM in traffic) means less oil pressure to keep the chain tensioned.
causes: Low-RPM city riding reducing oil pressure
fix_procedure: 1. Cold start rattle = CCT losing tension. Constant rattle = don't ride. 2. Convert to manual CCT (APE Pro Series, model-specific). 3. Check chain guides for wear. 4. Forum tip: the standard bike RPM profile is actually harder on the CCT than sport bike use — sport bikes spend more time at high RPM where oil pressure is strong. Standards lug around at 3000-4000 RPM in traffic where oil pressure is marginal. The manual CCT conversion is the same $40 fix regardless of which engine the bike uses. Make sure you buy the correct model-specific manual tensioner.

title: CB1000R throttle-by-wire hesitation and surge
description: The CB1000R (2008+) uses ride-by-wire throttle that can produce off-idle hesitation, surging at constant cruise, and abrupt throttle response in Sport mode. The issue is ECU mapping — the default map is aggressive for a naked commuter bike.
causes: ECU throttle mapping too aggressive in Sport mode
Intake air temperature sensor affecting mixture
fix_procedure: 1. Switch to Standard or Rain mode for city riding — Sport mode is too snatchy for traffic. 2. If surging at constant cruise: the ECU is hunting between load maps. Ride in a higher gear to keep the throttle opening more consistent. 3. Check for intake air leaks at throttle body boots. 4. If aftermarket exhaust installed, the ECU needs to relearn — ride 200+ miles in varied conditions. 5. Forum tip: the CB1000R throttle-by-wire is the same system as the CBR1000RR but detuned. It still has aggressive mapping that's designed for race-derived response. A Power Commander or Healtech QuickShifter with autotune smooths out the fueling significantly. Honda also released firmware updates for early CB1000R models — check with the dealer. The 2018+ Neo Sports Cafe version has improved mapping from factory.

title: Carburetor issues — CB750 Nighthawk (1991-2003)
description: The CB750 Nighthawk uses four Keihin CV carburetors that are sensitive to ethanol fuel and synchronization drift. After 20+ years, every CB750 needs carb work — pilot jets are clogged, diaphragms are cracked, and vacuum lines are deteriorated. The CB750 is a popular budget bike, so many come to shops in poor running condition.
causes: Pilot jet clogging from ethanol fuel
fix_procedure: 1. Remove carb rack (4 carbs as a unit). 2. Full teardown: float bowls, jets, needles, diaphragms. 3. Clean all jets with carb cleaner + compressed air. Focus on pilot jets and pilot air bleeds. 4. Replace diaphragms if cracked or stiff. 5. Check float height and float valve needles. 6. Sync with vacuum gauges after reassembly. 7. Forum tip: the CB750 Nighthawk is one of the most common 'barn find' bikes — people find them in garages, try to start them, and bring them to the shop when they run like garbage. Budget 3-4 hours for a full carb service. Buy a complete rebuild kit (K&L or Keyster, $40-60 for all 4 carbs). The CB750 carbs are well-documented — hundreds of YouTube videos exist for this specific bike. Also, while the carbs are off, check the airbox boots for cracks — they crack from heat and age and cause vacuum leaks.

title: Chain and sprocket wear — commuter use pattern
description: Standard/naked Hondas are primarily used as commuters, accumulating chain wear faster from stop-and-go city riding (frequent acceleration/deceleration loads). Many CB and Hornet owners neglect chain maintenance because the bikes don't have fairings that trap chain noise — the noise is lost in traffic.
causes: Frequent stop-start city riding loading chain
fix_procedure: 1. Check chain slack: 20-30mm free play at midpoint. 2. Check for tight spots by rotating wheel. 3. Inspect sprocket teeth for hooking. 4. Replace chain + both sprockets as a set if worn. 5. Use X-ring chain (DID, RK) for longer life. 6. Forum tip: standard/naked bikes eat chains faster than sport bikes because commuter riding = constant acceleration/deceleration loads. A sport bike cruising on the highway puts steady load on the chain; a commuter in traffic constantly varies the load. Lube every 500 miles minimum. An automatic chain oiler (Scott Oiler, $50) is the best mod for a daily commuter Honda. The CB1000R and newer Hornets use 525 chains; older models use 530.

title: Handlebar vibration — engine-to-chassis transfer
description: Naked Hondas with inline-4 engines transmit more vibration to the handlebars than their faired counterparts because there's no fairing mass to dampen high-frequency vibes. Riders experience hand numbness, tingling, and fatigue on rides over 30 minutes. Particularly bad on the CB919 and older Hornets.
causes: Inline-4 secondary vibrations transmitted through rigid bar mounts
No fairing mass to dampen vibration
Bar-end weights missing or wrong size
fix_procedure: 1. Check that bar-end weights are installed — they counterbalance handlebar vibration. Missing bar-ends dramatically increase vibes. 2. Check engine mount rubbers for deterioration — hardened mounts transmit more vibration. 3. Install bar-end weights (heavier = more damping). Aftermarket options from Rizoma, R&G, or BarkBusters. 4. Consider gel handlebar grips or foam grip donuts under grips. 5. Forum tip: the CB919 is particularly buzzy at 5000-6000 RPM because the firing order creates a secondary vibration that hits right at highway cruise RPM. Heavier bar-end weights ($20-40) are the single best fix. The CB1000R (2018+) has rubber-mounted handlebars that significantly reduce vibes — Honda finally addressed this. For older models, Oxford heated grips have a thick rubber layer that also dampens vibration. Also, check that the steering head bearings aren't loose — loose bearings amplify every vibration.

title: Fuel tank rust — older CB750 and Hornet models
description: Older Honda standard bikes stored with partially empty tanks develop internal rust. Rust flakes clog the fuel petcock (carb models), fuel filter, fuel pump strainer (EFI), and carburetor jets. The rust cycle: moisture condenses in empty tank space → rust forms → rust flakes contaminate fuel system → engine runs poorly or not at all.
causes: Lack of fuel stabilizer during storage
fix_procedure: 1. Drain tank and inspect inside with a flashlight. If orange/brown flakes or coating visible, the tank is rusted. 2. Minor surface rust: flush with acid-based tank cleaner (Kreem or POR-15 tank kit). 3. Heavy rust: use a mechanical agitator (nuts and bolts or BBs) with cleaner to knock loose rust off walls. 4. After cleaning, seal interior with tank sealer (Kreem or POR-15). 5. Replace fuel filter, clean carb jets or fuel injectors. 6. Forum tip: NEVER skip the sealer step after cleaning a rusty tank — bare metal will rust again in weeks. The POR-15 tank sealer kit ($40) includes cleaner, metal prep, and sealer — it's a one-shot fix if done correctly. The key is to get the tank completely dry before applying sealer. Use a hair dryer or compressed air. Also, to prevent future rust: keep the tank full during storage and add fuel stabilizer. A full tank has no air space for moisture to condense.

title: Headlight aim and visibility — naked bike riding position
description: Standard/naked bikes position the rider more upright, which changes the headlight aim relative to the road compared to a sport bike's tucked position. If the headlight was aimed for a sport bike rider (leaned forward), the beam points too high when sitting upright. Also, naked bikes lack fairing wind protection, and bugs/stone chips damage the headlight lens faster.
causes: Headlight aimed for sport bike position (too high for upright riding)
Headlight lens stone damage from no fairing protection
Headlight mounting vibration loosening aim screws
Low-wattage stock bulb insufficient for naked bike use
fix_procedure: 1. Re-aim headlight: on level ground, 25 feet from a wall, the low-beam cutoff line should be at or slightly below headlight center height. 2. Check aim adjustment screws — vibration loosens them over time. Apply thread-locker. 3. If lens is stone-pitted: replace lens or install a headlight protector (clear film or screen). 4. Upgrade to LED headlight for better illumination. 5. Forum tip: many used CB and Hornet models come from sport bike riders who converted to naked — the headlight was never re-aimed for the new riding position. A 5-minute aim adjustment dramatically improves night visibility. On the CB1000R (2018+), the LED headlight is excellent. On older models, an LED H4 conversion ($25-35) is the best night-riding upgrade. Also, consider adding auxiliary LED fog lights — naked bikes lack the fairing-mounted driving lights that touring bikes have.

title: Rear shock degradation — standard bike loads
description: Standard/naked Hondas carry riders in a more upright position, putting more weight on the rear suspension than a sport bike's forward weight distribution. The stock rear shock is often undersprung for the standard bike's weight distribution, and degradation happens faster. Commuter use with passenger or luggage accelerates the problem.
causes: More rider weight on rear from upright position
Stock shock undersprung for naked bike weight distribution
Shock oil degradation from age and heat
fix_procedure: 1. Check rear sag: with rider seated, compress should be 25-30% of total travel. More = shock is too soft or worn. 2. If shock body is weeping oil, it's blown — replace. 3. Adjust preload if adjustable (most Honda standards have preload adjustment). 4. Upgrade to aftermarket shock for significant improvement. 5. Forum tip: the stock rear shock on the CB919, CB750, and Hornet 600 is adequate for a lightweight solo rider on smooth roads. Add a passenger, some luggage, or a heavier rider, and it's overwhelmed. A YSS, Progressive, or Ohlins rear shock ($200-600) transforms the handling. For the CB1000R, the OEM shock is better than older models but still benefits from an upgrade for spirited riding. Set preload for your actual riding weight — don't leave it at factory settings.

title: Mirror vibration and blind spots — naked bike aerodynamics
description: Naked bike mirrors vibrate more than faired bike mirrors because there's no fairing to shield them from wind turbulence. At highway speeds (60+ mph), the mirrors become useless — the image vibrates into a blur. This is a safety issue because riders can't see traffic behind them.
causes: Engine vibration transmitted through bar-mounted mirrors
Incorrect mirror position compounding wind effects
fix_procedure: 1. First check that mirror stems are tight — loose stems amplify vibration. 2. Add mirror vibration dampeners (rubber isolator inserts, $10-15 per mirror). 3. Upgrade to bar-end mirrors — they're further from the engine vibration source and more aerodynamically stable. 4. Position mirrors so the flat of the mirror is perpendicular to wind flow at cruise speed. 5. Forum tip: bar-end mirrors are the #1 quality-of-life upgrade on any naked Honda. CRG Arrow or Rizoma bar-end mirrors ($50-100 each) provide a stable, usable view at any speed. The stock Honda mirrors on bar-mounted stalks are designed to clear regulations, not to actually be useful at speed. Another option: extend the mirror stems with spacers ($5) — getting the mirror higher and further out reduces the vibration zone. Also, convex/wide-angle mirror glass inserts increase the field of view without changing the mirror body.
